summary: DeVault traces his personal evolution from preferring the MIT license to embracing the GPL. He initially viewed copyleft as less free, and technically still does, but now sees that restriction as necessary and good. His daily immersion in Linux revealed how transformative it is when all software guarantees the four freedoms—reading source, contacting authors, fixing bugs, and sending patches became second nature. He argues that while the MIT license offers maximum theoretical freedom, the GPL is needed because capitalism incentivizes exploitation of free software labor. The GPL simply encodes the Golden Rule: reciprocate the freedoms you receive.
key_points: The GPL is technically less free than MIT, but that restriction serves a protective purpose for software freedom
Daily immersion in Linux makes the value of free software viscerally obvious—proprietary software feels like miscounting stairs in the dark
The collective effort behind free software amounts to tens of millions of hours, making it ripe for exploitation without copyleft protections
The GPL is the legal embodiment of the Golden Rule: extend others the same freedoms you benefited from
Anyone who can't agree to GPL's terms is looking to exploit your work for their gain, not yours
money_quotes: I still hold this opinion today: the GPL license is less free than the MIT license - but today, I believe this in a good way.
It radically changes your perspective when all of the software you use guarantees these four freedoms.
It's like miscounting the number of steps on your staircase in the dark.
Free software is good. It's starkly better than the alternative. And copyleft defends it. Now that I've had a taste, you bet your ass I'm not going to give it up.
Our socialist free software utopia is ripe for exploitation by capitalists, and they'll be rewarded for doing so.
The GPL is the legal embodiment of this Golden Rule: in exchange for benefiting from my hard work, you just have to extend me the same courtesy.
Anyone who can't agree to this is looking to exploit your work for their gain - and definitely not yours.
key_insight: The GPL's restrictions don't limit freedom—they defend it against a capitalist system that incentivizes exploitation of collective labor.
